items.prompt: Describe the recommended Premed subject journey to a new learner. Begin with scientific method and laboratory practice plus mathematics and statistics; include general chemistry, physics, organic chemistry, biochemistry, biology and genetics, human structure and physiology, microbiology and immunology, behaviour, society, and population health, and integration and capstone work. Explain at least two ways one stage prepares the next.

answer.correct: A complete response begins with inquiry, laboratory, and quantitative practices; moves through the chemical, physical, biological, and molecular foundations; carries them into human systems, microbes, immunity, behaviour, society, and populations; and ends with integration and capstone work. It explains at least two preparation links rather than giving only an unordered list.
answer.summary: The journey moves from trustworthy evidence and measurement through matter and living systems to people, populations, and an unfamiliar capstone problem.
answer.reasoning: Inquiry, laboratory, mathematics, and statistics come first because every later subject uses measurements and evidence. General chemistry, biology, and physics provide particle, cell, and physical explanations. Organic chemistry prepares molecular reasoning used in biochemistry; these foundations then support physiology, microbiology, immunity, and behavioural or population contexts. Integration and capstone work require selecting and connecting the relevant foundations. On retry, advise an incoming learner how to proceed from trustworthy evidence to a capstone rather than reproducing the original list.
acceptableResponses: Prose, bullets, or an outline that names every required subject area, preserves the broad recommended chronology, and explains at least two accurate preparation relationships.
commonErrors: Giving an unordered subject list with no explanation of why the order helps
Omitting behaviour, society, population health, or integration and capstone work
Beginning with advanced medical content instead of evidence and measurement

items.prompt: Choose four representative concepts from four different Premed subject areas. In plain language, explain what each concept helps unlock for later medical study. You are describing relevance, not teaching the detailed science.

answer.correct: The response identifies four representative concepts from four different subject areas and gives a defensible later-study link for each, such as uncertainty to research interpretation, pressure and flow to circulation, molecular shape to biomolecule behaviour, gene expression to cell differences, or social conditions to population health patterns.
answer.summary: Representative concepts earn their place by enabling later explanations, measurements, or evidence judgments.
answer.reasoning: This task asks for a concept-to-purpose relationship. Naming a subject is not enough; each example must say what the concept later helps explain or evaluate. A retry should use different subjects or concepts so that the learner reconstructs the relationship instead of copying feedback.
acceptableResponses: Any four accurate concept-and-relevance pairs drawn from four different named subject areas in the lesson.
commonErrors: Listing concepts without saying what they unlock
Making clinical claims instead of describing preparation for study
Using four concepts from only one subject

items.prompt: A learner says that completing Premed means remembering a large set of facts. Correct that account. State the four main completion capabilities and give one concrete action that shows each capability.

answer.correct: Completion requires explaining scientific relationships, modelling them with appropriate representations, investigating questions with auditable methods, and integrating several subjects in unfamiliar problems. Each capability must be tied to an observable action.
answer.summary: Premed aims at explanation, modelling, investigation, and integration rather than recall alone.
answer.reasoning: Facts support the four capabilities but do not replace them. Concrete evidence includes explaining a relationship, using and limiting a graph or model, designing and documenting a fair investigation, and connecting several domains in a new problem. On retry, apply the four capabilities to a new non-diagnostic capstone scenario rather than repeating their definitions.
acceptableResponses: Equivalent wording that identifies explain, model, investigate, and integrate and gives an observable example of each.
commonErrors: Treating course completion as recall or time spent
Naming the four verbs without concrete actions
Claiming that completion grants clinical competence or professional authority

criteria.description: Begins with scientific method, laboratory practice, mathematics, and statistics as shared foundations.
Includes the chemical, physical, biological, genetic, organic, and biochemical subjects in a defensible broad order.
Includes human structure and physiology, microbiology and immunology, behaviour, society, population health, and integration and capstone work.
Explains at least two accurate ways one stage prepares later study.
Selects four representative concepts from four different subject areas.
Explains an accurate later-study purpose for each selected concept.
Uses plain language and stays at orientation depth without unsupported clinical claims.
Identifies explaining, modelling, investigating, and integrating.
Gives one observable scientific action for each capability.
Distinguishes scientific preparation from recall alone and from clinical or professional authority.
